title: Run non-delivering ads in separate campaigns to unlock different audiences
insight: Meta's winner-takes-all delivery means 1-2 ads get all the spend while others are starved. This is bad for audience expansion because the winning ad may only be best for a specific audience. When non-delivering ads target different user personas or suit different placements (e.g., static vs UGC video), run them in parallel campaigns rather than the same ad set. Ideally, restrict by placement — video-only on Reels, statics-only on Feed — so formats don't compete against each other. Having 2-3 campaigns running in parallel is fine; 10 is too many.
raw_excerpt: It's clear there's a winner takes all ads all the time, but I think it's bad decision from Meta to deliver this way because it prevents audience expansion... It's quite common from a certain scale to actually run in parallel with the other creative.
source_title: Mobile Dev Memo S6E1: MDM Mailbag #3 (with Thomas Petit)
actionable_steps: If promising ads aren't getting delivery, run them in a separate parallel campaign rather than forcing spend
Split by placement: video ads in Reels-only campaigns, static ads in Feed-only campaigns
Limit parallel campaigns to 2-3 maximum to avoid fragmentation
Accept that Meta's delivery decision is often correct — forced delivery may just confirm the loser

title: Two-step creative testing: traction test then funnel validation
insight: Thomas Petit uses a two-step creative testing process. Step 1 (Traction): put ~50 creatives in with minimal budget and see which ones Meta wants to spend on first. Don't look at CPI or any downstream metric — just which ads get the first $100 of spend. This quickly eliminates losers for $0 or near-$0. Step 2 (Validation): take the 3-5 survivors and put more budget on them to validate they convert through the full funnel (installs, trials, revenue). Only after passing both tests do creatives enter the BAU campaign.
raw_excerpt: I put 50 creative together and I look at on which one Facebook wants to spend the first hundred dollar... Once a creative can deliver, I'm gonna pass to a validation testing phase where I put more budget and validate that they don't trash my funnel... only then I put it in the main campaign.
source_title: Mobile Dev Memo S6E1: MDM Mailbag #3 (with Thomas Petit)
actionable_steps: Set up a low-budget traction test campaign: load ~50 creatives and watch which ones Meta spends on first
Kill losers after $0-100 spend — don't force equal budget on every creative
Graduate 3-5 traction winners to a validation campaign with more budget to test full-funnel conversion
Only promote creatives to BAU campaigns after they pass both traction and funnel validation

title: Never force equal spend on creative testing — let Meta's delivery signal decide
insight: The worst creative testing method is forcing equal spend on every ad (e.g., $500 each). This wastes massive budget on losers. Creative testing should be about eliminating losers as fast as possible, not searching for winners. If Meta doesn't want to spend on an ad, that's a signal — the ad likely has near-zero probability of converting at scale. Kill it after $0 spent. The platform's delivery decision is itself a quality signal because it's informed by first-party engagement data.
raw_excerpt: They force spend on every single ad... this is the worst you can do. You're trashing a ton of budget like the losers. You want to eliminate them, sometimes after $0 spent. Meta doesn't want to spend, that's it, bye-bye.
source_title: Mobile Dev Memo S6E1: MDM Mailbag #3 (with Thomas Petit)
actionable_steps: Never allocate equal budget to all creatives in a test — this is the worst approach
Use Meta's delivery as the first quality filter: if it won't spend, kill the creative immediately
Treat creative testing as an exercise in eliminating losers quickly, not finding winners slowly
Anonymize creatives during testing — look at performance data before viewing the creative itself

title: Learning phase requires 10+ events per day minimum — stop campaigns below that
insight: Meta's learning phase requires approximately 50 events per week (~7/day), but Thomas recommends a minimum of 10 events per day as the practical threshold. If a campaign can only produce 2-3 events per day, it will never stabilize — no machine learning can work with that few data points. The solutions are: increase budget, change to a higher-volume optimization event (e.g., from purchase to trial start), or don't run the campaign. Waiting 25 days for 50 events is never the right answer.
raw_excerpt: I try to never run anything that doesn't have the potential to reach about 10 events per day... the case you're describing, if it needs to wait for 25 days to have 50 events, I don't think it's ever going to work. It's ever going to stabilize.
source_title: Mobile Dev Memo S6E1: MDM Mailbag #3 (with Thomas Petit)
actionable_steps: Calculate if your budget and conversion rate can produce 10+ optimization events per day
If not reaching 10 events/day, increase budget OR switch to a higher-volume event (e.g., trial instead of purchase)
Never wait 20+ days hoping a low-volume campaign stabilizes — it won't
Kill campaigns that can't reach the event volume threshold and reallocate budget

title: ASO is a must-do baseline, not a growth strategy — keywords have lost importance
insight: ASO as a standalone growth strategy is dead. Keywords have lost importance, and no app can scale on ASO alone. However, the product page (icon, screenshots, value proposition) is a must-do because every single user passes through it regardless of acquisition source — paid, organic, referral, or PR. Iterate on product pages to improve conversion, but don't expect it to drive discovery. CPPs (Custom Product Pages) tied to paid UA are valuable. Thomas stopped selling ASO services in 2017 because he couldn't produce systematic impact at scale.
raw_excerpt: I stopped selling ASO services in 2017... I couldn't see that the value, the impact I'm bringing can compensate for the money I ask in a reliable way... every single user, no matter where they come from, are going to go through that step before they enter your product. I do think you have to take care of it.
source_title: Mobile Dev Memo S6E1: MDM Mailbag #3 (with Thomas Petit)
actionable_steps: Treat your App Store product page as a conversion optimization exercise, not a discovery strategy
Regularly A/B test icon, screenshots, and value proposition copy
Use Custom Product Pages (CPPs) to match paid UA messaging with post-click experience
Don't invest in keyword optimization as a primary growth lever — it has diminishing returns

title: View-through ASA conversions: never include in reporting, but useful for 1 diagnostic
insight: Never include Apple Search Ads view-through conversions in attribution reporting — it's complete inflation, especially on the 2-day tap-through attribution window. However, there's one diagnostic use case: on brand defense campaigns, comparing tap-through + view-through totals before and after a store page change. If CTR drops but total installs (taps + views) stay the same, the new store page is actually winning — fewer paid clicks means more users are converting organically from the listing, saving you money.
raw_excerpt: Never, ever. Don't do view-through on search... But there is one case — by looking at the view-through conversion, I can see the sum of the two. Less people were clicking on the ad, but the same amount of installs were coming. Lower CTR, I'm paying less, and I'm still getting the installs. It's a win.
source_title: Mobile Dev Memo S6E1: MDM Mailbag #3 (with Thomas Petit)
actionable_steps: Exclude ASA view-through conversions from all attribution reporting
Use view-through data only for diagnostics: compare total installs (tap + view) before/after store page changes
If brand defense CTR drops but total installs hold steady, the new store page is driving organic installs — it's a win

title: Demographic targeting is largely irrelevant — use creative and signal filtering instead
insight: Demographic targeting (age, gender) on ad platforms is largely irrelevant for UA. Many networks don't support it, and even where available (Meta), it's unreliable. Instead, control who engages through: (1) creative messaging — change who the ad appeals to by changing the content, (2) signal filtering — exclude unwanted segments by filtering conversion events sent back to the platform. For example, if users under 21 have poor trial conversion, include an age question in onboarding and filter out under-21 events from the optimization signal so the platform learns to avoid them.
raw_excerpt: I believe demographic targeting is largely irrelevant... What I did is I'm filtering out the events going back to Google for people who are below a certain age so that their platform would end up learning that I don't want these users.
source_title: Mobile Dev Memo S6E1: MDM Mailbag #3 (with Thomas Petit)
actionable_steps: Don't rely on platform demographic targeting — it's unreliable or unavailable on most networks
Use creative messaging to self-select the right audience rather than demographic restrictions
Filter conversion events by excluding unwanted segments (e.g., under-21) from signals sent to ad platforms
Add demographic questions in onboarding to enable server-side signal filtering for optimization

title: Non-gaming iOS channel mix: Meta 30-60%, AppLovin 20-25%, TikTok 10-20%, Apple 5-10%
insight: For non-gaming subscription apps spending $100K-$2M/month, the iOS channel mix typically looks like: Meta 30-60% (still #1 but less dominant than 5 years ago), AppLovin 20-25% (rapidly growing from near-zero 2 years ago), TikTok 10-20%, Apple Search Ads 5-10% (always in that range due to search-only nature), Google negligible on iOS. On Android, Google dominates with 50%+ market share, often approaching 100% for some apps. AppLovin is the gateway to ad networks — most apps start there before testing others.
raw_excerpt: Meta is still the king... 30 and 60% of the iOS budget going to meta. Apple is always 5 or 10% but it's never less and it's never more... AppLovin gaining 20-25% of budget. TikTok rarely more than 10% or 20%. Google is meaningless on iOS.
source_title: Mobile Dev Memo S6E1: MDM Mailbag #3 (with Thomas Petit)
actionable_steps: Prioritize Meta as primary iOS channel, allocating 30-60% of iOS budget
Test AppLovin as second channel — it's scaled to 20-25% of budget for many subscription apps
Add TikTok as third channel at 10-20% of budget
Keep Apple Search Ads at 5-10% as steady baseline — don't expect it to scale beyond that
On Android, lean into Google as dominant channel (50%+ of spend)

title: Small platform syndrome: must exceed Meta ROAS plus onboarding cost to justify adoption
insight: Smaller ad platforms (Snap, Pinterest, Reddit, X) face 'small platform syndrome' — they don't just need to match Meta's ROAS, they need to exceed it by whatever magnitude covers the headcount, engineering, data integration, and CAPI setup costs of onboarding them. Even if they slightly exceed Meta's ROAS, the incremental management burden may not justify the investment. The diminishing returns of adding channels means the marginal dollar is often better spent scaling an existing platform than onboarding a new one.
raw_excerpt: They don't have to match Meta's ROAS. They have to exceed Meta's ROAS by whatever magnitude the headache of onboarding them is... Even if they exceeded a little bit, it still might not be worth the effort.
source_title: Mobile Dev Memo S6E1: MDM Mailbag #3 (with Thomas Petit)
actionable_steps: Before onboarding a new ad platform, calculate the full cost: headcount, engineering, data integration, ongoing management
The new platform's ROAS must exceed your best existing channel's ROAS by more than the onboarding cost
Consider scaling existing channels further before adding new ones — diminishing returns are real but often less painful
Limit your active channel count to 3-5 maximum for most subscription apps

title: App-to-web monetization favors big brands — mixed results for smaller apps
insight: Testing app-to-web monetization (redirecting users to web for payment to avoid Apple's 30% fee) shows mixed results. Bigger brands benefit most because brand recognition helps convert users to enter credit card details on an external website. Smaller apps struggle because users don't trust unfamiliar brands enough to pay outside the App Store. Below $1M annual revenue (Small Business Program at 15% fee), the savings don't justify the complexity. Many developers are deliberately waiting for legal/regulatory dust to settle before investing, as Apple may retaliate via reduced organic visibility as seen in Europe.
raw_excerpt: I'm seeing mixed results. It tends to be that the bigger ones are profiting the most, probably because of brand awareness... I've seen a lot of people fail at this. The smaller people under 1,000,000, the win is not going to be big enough to justify it.
source_title: Mobile Dev Memo S6E1: MDM Mailbag #3 (with Thomas Petit)
actionable_steps: If under $1M annual revenue (15% Apple fee tier), don't invest in app-to-web monetization — the savings aren't worth it
If large brand with strong recognition, test app-to-web for high-value users who are most likely to convert on web
Not all users should be routed to web — build logic to determine which user segments convert better on web vs in-app
Monitor Apple's policy changes and potential organic visibility retaliation before going all-in

title: AI creative value is speed and volume, not cost savings — reinvest savings into more production
insight: The primary value of AI creative tools is producing more creatives faster, not producing them cheaper. Teams that were testing 5 ads are now testing 50; those testing 50 are testing 2000. Even if production is 40x cheaper, the savings get reinvested into 40x more production. AI-produced video has just reached the tipping point of being actually usable in ads. Within 6 months, it will be obvious to everyone that AI is the dominant creative production method. However, the real underexplored frontier is AI for concept ideation and data analysis, not just production.
raw_excerpt: Producing more and faster was even more important than producing cheaper... even if it's 40 times cheaper, you're going to reinvest this money into actually producing 40 times more. So I'm not sure that the cost saving is the driver.
source_title: Mobile Dev Memo S6E1: MDM Mailbag #3 (with Thomas Petit)
actionable_steps: Frame AI creative tools as a volume multiplier, not a cost savings tool
Reinvest any production cost savings into producing more creative variations
Explore AI for concept ideation and performance data analysis — these are underexplored high-value use cases
Start experimenting with AI video production now — it just crossed the usability threshold

title: AI for performance analytics is hugely underexplored — spotting micro-segments in real time
insight: AI applied to creative production dominates the conversation, but the bigger untapped opportunity is AI for performance analytics. No data analyst can monitor every segment, funnel step, country, platform, creative, and channel combination simultaneously. AI could surface micro-segments that deteriorated overnight — like a 2% spend segment that dropped performance but went unnoticed because it's small on a percentage basis but significant in absolute dollars. MMPs, analytics tools (Amplitude, Firebase), and BI systems have been slow to integrate AI for anomaly detection and pattern recognition.
raw_excerpt: Who's better than AI to tell me, hey, here, there's a tiny segment that just got worse yesterday, and you didn't spot it because it's like 2% of your spend, but 2% of many millions is still significant... I'm surprised we're not there yet.
source_title: Mobile Dev Memo S6E1: MDM Mailbag #3 (with Thomas Petit)
actionable_steps: Explore AI-powered anomaly detection for campaign performance data
Build or adopt tools that can alert on micro-segment performance changes across all dimensions
Don't limit AI adoption to creative production — performance analytics is the bigger unlock
